title: Lesson 2 — Composition
_authoring: Pedagogical full pass (curriculum audit). Added intro with lessonIntro, per-KC explanations (composition, XOR-as-composition), and a worked example ((A OR B) AND C traced row-by-row). Bridge items at the front ease into 3-variable composition. Owner-directed 2026-06-03: the FIRST item is now l2-bridge-and-b (plain A AND B, tier 1) — the simplest possible circuit, a single AND gate — so the composition lesson starts from the gate the learner already mastered before introducing NOT-inside-composition (A AND NOT B). On a circuit challenge the learner can tap the 0/1 chip on each input to change A and B and watch the output follow the gate (the circuit-prompt copy says so); this single-AND opener is where that exploration is most legible. Then l2-bridge-and-notb and l2-nor (both promoted to tier 1). Interleaved XOR discrimination anchor (l2-xor-canonical) earlier — moved from tier 3 position into mid-sequence after bridge items establish OR/NOT composition. Final tier ordering: bridge items (tier 1 2-var) → simple 3-var compositions (tier 1) → NOT-inside-composition (tier 2) → XOR canonical anchor (tier 2) → XOR forms + complex composition (tier 3) → majority + 3-var XOR (tier 4). All truthTables verified MSB-first. XOR is a pedagogical label only — never written as 'A XOR B' in expressions. Gate-reachability fix: added l2-comp3-ab-or-notanot c as a third tier-4 item — mastery_config requires consecutiveCorrectAtHardestTier:3 but there were only 2 tier-4 items, making the gate mathematically unreachable. Three tier-4 items are now present: l2-majority, l2-xor-three, l2-comp3-aob-nac.

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 2 — Composition: Building Larger Expressions
lessonIntro.body:
You can wire the operators from Lesson 1 together to describe any condition imaginable. Composition means the output of one operator becomes the input to another. The key skill is evaluation order: work inside parentheses first, then outward — exactly like arithmetic. In this lesson you will build truth tables for compound expressions and meet XOR ('exactly one input true'), which is not a new gate but a composition of AND, OR, and NOT.

By the end you'll be able to complete the truth table for a compound expression by evaluating it in the right order — innermost group first — including XOR's signature [0,1,1,0] pattern. You've succeeded when you can fill in a two- or three-variable compound table correctly, no hints, and respect precedence (NOT, then AND, then OR) without being told.

explanations.body: A composite expression nests one Boolean expression inside another. Evaluate the innermost sub-expression first, then use its result as the input to the outer operator. Parentheses force a grouping; without them, NOT binds tightest, then AND, then OR — exactly like multiplication before addition. Example: NOT A OR B means (NOT A) OR B, not NOT (A OR B).

XOR ('exclusive or') means exactly one input is true — true when A is true and B is false, or when B is true and A is false, but NOT when both are true (unlike inclusive OR). XOR is not a separate gate in our grammar; it is the composition (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B). The truth table [0,1,1,0] is its fingerprint — check any expression claiming to be XOR against it.

steps.label: Evaluate the inner sub-expression (A OR B) for each row
Apply AND with C
steps.detail: Now AND each sub-expression result with C (the last variable in each row tuple). (000): 0 AND 0 = 0. (001): 0 AND 1 = 0. (010): 1 AND 0 = 0. (011): 1 AND 1 = 1. (100): 1 AND 0 = 0. (101): 1 AND 1 = 1. (110): 1 AND 0 = 0. (111): 1 AND 1 = 1.

steps.label: Read the output column
steps.detail: Final table for (A OR B) AND C: [0,0,0,1,0,1,0,1]. C acts as a gate — the sub-expression result only passes through when C=1.

items.targetExpression: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B)
(A OR B) AND NOT (A AND B)
(NOT A AND B) OR (A AND NOT B)
(A AND B) OR (NOT A AND NOT B)
(A AND B) OR (B AND C) OR (A AND C)
(A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B) OR C
(A AND B) OR (NOT A AND NOT C)
